title: ब्रह्मा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री ब्रह्मा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रथमं ब्रह्ममित्युक्तं द्वितीयं विधातृकम्।
तृतीयं प्रजापतिं चैव चतुर्थं पद्मजं विदुः॥१॥

पञ्चमं हिरण्यगर्भं च षष्ठं पितामहं तथा।
सप्तमं चतुर्मुखं नाम अष्टमं सृष्टिकर्तृकम्॥२॥

नवमं वेदगर्भं च दशमं कमलासनम्।
एकादशं स्वयम्भूतं द्वादशं लोकपितामहम्॥३॥

द्वादशैतानि नामानि ब्रह्मणो विधिनां पतेः।
यः पठेत् प्रातरुत्थाय सर्वविद्या प्रसिध्यति॥४॥

सृजनशक्तिमाप्नोति बुद्धिमेधा च वर्धते।
सन्तानसौख्यं लभते ब्रह्मकृपा निरन्तरम्॥५॥

॥ इति श्री ब्रह्मा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Brahma Dwadash Nama Stotram ||

Prathamam Brahmam-ityuktam Dvitiyam Vidhatrikam.
Tritiyam Prajapatim Chaiva Chaturtham Padmajam Viduh.||1||

Panchamam Hiranya-garbham Cha Shashtham Pitamaham Tatha.
Saptamam Chatur-mukham Nama Ashtamam Srishti-kartrikam.||2||

Navamam Veda-garbham Cha Dashamam Kamala-asanam.
Ekadasham Svayam-bhutam Dvadasham Loka-pitamaham.||3||

Dvadashai-tani Namani Brahmano Vidhinam Pateh.
Yah Pathet Pratar-utthaya Sarva-vidya Prasidhyati.||4||

Srijana-shaktim-apnoti Buddhi-medha Cha Vardhate.
Santana-saukhyam Labhate Brahma-kripa Nirantaram.||5||

|| Iti Shri Brahma Dwadash Nama Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रथम नाम ब्रह्मा, दूसरा विधाता, तीसरा प्रजापति, चौथा पद्मज। पाँचवाँ हिरण्यगर्भ, छठा पितामह, सातवाँ चतुर्मुख, आठवाँ सृष्टिकर्ता। नवाँ वेदगर्भ, दसवाँ कमलासन, ग्यारहवाँ स्वयम्भू, बारहवाँ लोकपितामह। ब्रह्मा के इन बारह नामों का प्रातःकाल पाठ करने से सर्वविद्या प्रसिद्ध होती है, सृजनशक्ति प्राप्त होती है, बुद्धि-मेधा बढ़ती है और सन्तानसुख मिलता है।
benefits: Twelve names of Lord Brahma, the creator of the universe
Invokes mastery over all forms of knowledge and learning
Enhances creative power, intelligence, and wisdom
Invokes the blessing of happiness through progeny and family
Traditionally recited to invoke continuous grace of brahma deva
description: Brahma Dwadash Nama Stotram — ब्रह्मा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् — is a devotional hymn of praise offered to Lord Brahma. Rooted in Brahma Purana, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Regular recitation is believed to grant mastery over all forms of knowledge and learning, ensure continuous grace of Brahma Deva, and enhance creative power, intelligence, and wisdom. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Wednesday (Budhvar) and Thursday (Guruvar). At 5 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 3 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Brahma Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Recite Brahma Dwadash Nama - 12 names of Lord Brahma in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning for knowledge, creativity, intelligence, and progeny.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Brahma Dwadasha Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Brahma Dwadasha Nama Stotram (ब्रह्मा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् — Hymn of the Twelve Names of Brahma) is a short Sanskrit stotra enumerating the twelve sacred names of Lord Brahma — the Creator-deity of the Hindu Trimurti, husband of Goddess Saraswati, and grandfather (Pitamaha) of all beings. The hymn lists: (1) Brahma — the expanded one, (2) Vidhata — the dispenser, (3) Prajapati — lord of all creatures, (4) Padmaja — lotus-born (born from Vishnu's navel-lotus), (5) Hiranyagarbha — the golden cosmic womb (Rigveda 10.121), (6) Pitamaha — grandfather of the universe, (7) Chaturmukha — four-faced one, (8) Srishtikartri — the Creator, (9) Vedagarbha — the womb of Vedas, (10) Kamalasana — lotus-seated, (11) Svayambhu — self-born, (12) Lokapitamaha — grandfather of the worlds. The phala-shruti promises that one who recites these twelve names at sunrise attains all desires. The text is preserved in classical Brahma-upasana tradition. (Sources: Skanda Purana / Padma Purana; Brahma stotra collections.)

seoFaqs.question: Why are there exactly twelve names of Brahma in this stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The number twelve is highly significant in Hindu cosmology — twelve Adityas (sons of Aditi who become the twelve solar months), twelve months of the year, twelve rashis of the zodiac, twelve jyotirlingas of Shiva, twelve names of Vishnu, twelve names of Surya for Surya Namaskar. Twelve represents cosmic completeness and full annual cycle. By assigning twelve names to Brahma, the stotra invokes Brahma as the creator who works through all twelve months of the cosmic year and all twelve aspects of solar-creative time. Each of the twelve names also captures a distinct facet of Brahma's creative function: his birth from Vishnu's lotus (Padmaja), his Vedic source (Vedagarbha), his self-existent nature (Svayambhu), his four-faced cosmic vision (Chaturmukha), his role as universal grandfather (Pitamaha, Lokapitamaha). Reciting all twelve names in morning recitation invokes the complete creative power of Brahma. (Sources: Hindu numerological tradition; Padma Purana Brahma stotras.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the meanings of Brahma's twelve names?
seoFaqs.answer: Each Brahma name reveals a distinct aspect: (1) Brahma — derived from brih (to expand), the One who expanded into creation. (2) Vidhata — the cosmic Dispenser who allots life, karma, and destiny. (3) Prajapati — Lord of all praja (creatures), the cosmic father. (4) Padmaja — Lotus-born, referring to his emergence from the lotus that grew from Vishnu's navel during Yoga-nidra. (5) Hiranyagarbha — the Golden Cosmic Womb / Egg of the famous Rigveda 10.121 hymn that opens with Hiranyagarbhah Samavartataagre. (6) Pitamaha — Grandfather (literally father's father), since Brahma is father of Marichi-Atri-Bhrigu rishis who in turn fathered all beings. (7) Chaturmukha — Four-faced (each face for one Veda — Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva). (8) Srishtikartri — the Creator-Doer. (9) Vedagarbha — Womb of the Vedas (which emerged from his four mouths). (10) Kamalasana — Lotus-seated. (11) Svayambhu — Self-existent. (12) Lokapitamaha — Grandfather of all worlds. (Sources: Sanskrit etymological tradition; Padma Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting Brahma Dwadasha Nama Stotram daily?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti of the stotra (the closing benefits-declaration verse) promises that one who recites these twelve names at sunrise (pratah-uthaaya) obtains all desires (sarva-kamartha-siddhi). Specific benefits include: (1) Knowledge and wisdom — Brahma is the creator of Vedas, his blessing grants jnana. (2) Education success — students preparing for exams, scholars, researchers, and writers benefit specifically. (3) Saraswati grace — since Brahma's consort is the goddess of learning, his upasana also pleases Saraswati. (4) Creative success — for artists, designers, architects, engineers, and inventors who do creative work. (5) Karma understanding — Brahma as Vidhata grants insight into one's karmic patterns. (6) Removal of birth-chart afflictions related to creation, fertility, and writing-speech. (7) Ancestor-blessing — Brahma as Pitamaha pleases the entire pitru-lineage. The recitation is especially beneficial during Brahma Muhurta (pre-dawn). (Sources: Stotra phala-shruti; Pushkar Brahma temple paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Brahma rarely worshipped in independent temples?
seoFaqs.answer: Although Brahma is one of the Trimurti (Brahma-Vishnu-Mahesh), independent Brahma temples are extremely rare in India — the most famous being the Pushkar Brahma temple in Rajasthan (one of only a few worldwide). The Padma Purana explains this through a curse story. During a yajna at Pushkar, Brahma needed his consort Saraswati but she was delayed. To complete the ritual on time, Brahma married a Gujjar girl named Gayatri and seated her beside him. When Saraswati arrived and saw another woman in her place, she cursed Brahma that despite being the creator he would not be worshipped in any temple on earth — except at Pushkar where the offence occurred. Other versions involve a curse from Sage Bhrigu or from Shiva. Despite the rarity of temples, Brahma is daily invoked in every Hindu Sandhya Vandana, every yajna (as Brahma — the silent presiding priest), and through stotras like the Dwadasha Nama. (Sources: Padma Purana Srishti Khanda; Pushkar Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should Brahma Dwadasha Nama Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti specifies pratah-uthaaya — recitation immediately after waking, ideally during Brahma Muhurta (the auspicious pre-dawn period from approximately 4-5:30 AM that bears Brahma's own name). Special days for emphasized recitation include: every full moon (Purnima) since Brahma is associated with Kartik Purnima at Pushkar (the supreme Brahma festival with the Pushkar fair), Vasant Panchami (Magha Shukla 5 — Saraswati Jayanti, since Brahma's consort is honored), every Vyatipata yoga day (associated with Brahma in panchanga), Akshaya Tritiya, all yajna days, every Ekadashi, and during Chaturmasya (especially Shravan-Bhadrapada). The mantra is recited 12 times (one per name), 21, 51, or 108 times facing east at sunrise, with white flowers (Brahma's color), white sandalwood, and a kalash of pure water. Especially recommended for students, scholars, writers, and creative professionals. (Sources: Pushkar paddhati; Brahma upasana tradition.)
